title: Reproducible chest wall pain in a higher-risk man with sympathomimetic exposure

chart.oneLiner: 38M with GERD, panic disorder, hypertension, and a strong family history of early coronary disease presents with 3 days of sharp left-sided chest pain that began after an intense weightlifting session, worse with movement and deep breaths, on a background of an OTC decongestant binge and recent cocaine use.

opening: I've got this sharp pain on the left side of my chest. It started after I was lifting hard at the gym a few days ago, but with my dad's heart history I got scared and came in.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 38-year-old man, healthy-appearing but anxious, frightened he is having a heart attack like his father did at 45. Cooperative but wound up; tends to focus on his fear of cardiac death.

HPI:
- Sharp, well-LOCALIZED pain over the left anterior chest for 3 days.
- Began after a heavy chest workout (bench press, heavy flys) at the gym (only mentions the gym if asked what he was doing when it started).
- Worse with MOVEMENT, twisting, reaching overhead, and DEEP BREATHS; better with rest and staying still. Lying flat after big meals gives a separate burning behind the breastbone (his usual reflux) that he can tell apart from the sharp pain.
- He can point to the exact spot with one finger and pressing on it reproduces the SHARP pain.
- NO exertional pattern with walking or climbing stairs, NO crushing pressure/heaviness, NO diaphoresis, NO radiation to arm/jaw, NO dyspnea at rest, NO syncope, NO fever, NO cough, NO recent long travel or leg swelling, NO palpitations at rest.
- Around the time it started he had been hammering an over-the-counter pseudoephedrine decongestant for a head cold (well over the label dose for two days) AND used cocaine at a party that weekend — he is embarrassed and will only disclose the cocaine if asked specifically and non-judgmentally about recreational/stimulant drug use. He felt jittery and his heart raced that night, but the racing settled; the sharp chest-wall pain is what persists now.

OLDCARTS: onset 3 days ago right after exercise; left anterior chest, pinpoint; intermittent; sharp/stabbing; worse with movement, palpation, and deep breath; partly relieved by rest and ibuprofen; severity 5/10. The reflux burning is a separate, familiar, dull retrosternal symptom after meals.

PMH: GERD; panic disorder/generalized anxiety; essential hypertension; mild seasonal allergic rhinitis. No known coronary disease, no diabetes, no prior chest pain like this.
MEDICATIONS: omeprazole 40 mg daily (GERD); sertraline 100 mg daily (anxiety); hydroxyzine 25 mg as needed for anxiety/itch (an anticholinergic; he took several extra doses this week); amlodipine 5 mg daily (hypertension, frequently forgets it); OTC pseudoephedrine (took well above label dose for 2 days); ibuprofen as needed for the chest pain; occasional cocaine (recreational, weekends).
ALLERGIES: none. 
FAMILY: father had an MI at 45 and died of a second MI at 58; mother with hypertension. This family history is why he is so frightened.
SOCIAL: works in construction, lifts weights regularly, drinks 3-4 beers on weekends, smokes about half a pack a day for 15 years, occasional cocaine as above.
ROS: chest pain as above; the only positives are the chest-wall pain, the separate reflux burning, and feeling jittery/anxious; otherwise negative — no fever, cough, leg swelling, calf pain, melena, or weight loss.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: well-appearing, anxious, comfortable at rest, no distress.
- Vitals: BP 152/94, HR 98, RR 16, 36.9 C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Chest wall: REPRODUCIBLE point tenderness over the left 4th-5th costochondral junctions; firm palpation recreates his exact sharp pain. Mild tenderness of the pectoral muscle belly.
- Cardiac: regular, normal S1/S2, no murmur, no rub, no gallop. No JVD.
- Lungs: clear bilaterally, good air entry, no wheeze.
- Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness, symmetric pulses, no track marks.
- Abdomen: soft, mildly tender in the epigastrium (consistent with his reflux), no rebound.
- Neuro: alert, no tremor at rest, no asterixis.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the gym/weightlifting trigger unless asked what brought it on or what he was doing.
- Do NOT volunteer the cocaine use unless asked specifically and respectfully about recreational/stimulant/street-drug use; if asked judgmentally, become guarded and deny. Do NOT volunteer the pseudoephedrine overuse unless asked about OTC/cold medicines or what else he has been taking.
- The reproducible tenderness is only revealed if the student palpates the chest wall.
- The epigastric tenderness is only revealed if the abdomen is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin NORMAL and unchanged on a 0- and 3-hour delta (e.g. 8 ng/L then 9 ng/L, both below the assay's upper reference limit) - no myocardial injury; argues strongly against acute coronary syndrome / cocaine-associated MI.
labs.CBC: WBC 7.8, Hgb 15.2, Plt 244 - normal; no leukocytosis to suggest infection or large inflammatory process.
labs.BMP: Na 139, K 3.1 (LOW), Cl 104, HCO3 24, BUN 14, Cr 1.0, glucose 96. Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 139 - (104 + 24) = 11 (normal). The isolated HYPOKALEMIA (K 3.1) is from the sympathomimetic surge (cocaine + high-dose pseudoephedrine drive potassium intracellularly) and explains the U waves on the EKG; renal function and acid-base status are otherwise normal.
labs.CK: Creatine kinase mildly elevated at 480 U/L (upper reference ~200) - consistent with the heavy unaccustomed weightlifting (skeletal-muscle, not cardiac, source); the normal troponin confirms this is musculoskeletal, not myocardial.
labs.UDS: Urine drug screen POSITIVE for cocaine metabolite (benzoylecgonine); negative for amphetamines, opiates, benzodiazepines - corroborates the recent cocaine use and the sympathomimetic picture.
labs.Dimer: D-dimer NORMAL (not elevated) - low pretest probability PE further lowered; no further PE imaging indicated.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). This man has a frightening family history and sympathomimetic exposure, so explicitly name at least three dangerous mechanisms/etiologies of chest pain you must consider and exclude before settling on a benign cause.
steps.rubric: Musculoskeletal chest pain / costochondritis (leading,7; accepts chest wall strain after heavy lifting). The KEY competing diagnosis: cocaine/sympathomimetic-associated acute coronary syndrome or myocardial ischemia - genuinely on the table given cocaine + high-dose pseudoephedrine + smoking + strong family history of early MI + hypertension; must be actively excluded, not dismissed (CANT-MISS,6). Names at least three dangerous mechanisms/etiologies to exclude - reward each: cocaine/sympathomimetic ACS or coronary vasospasm; aortic dissection (sympathomimetic + uncontrolled BP); pulmonary embolism; pericarditis/myocarditis; pneumothorax after heavy exertion (any three credited, core,4 total). Names costochondritis with supporting features - reproducible point tenderness, exercise trigger, sharp/pleuritic-with-movement quality (core,4). Recognizes GERD and panic as plausible non-cardiac contributors given his PMH/meds (core,2). PENALTY: anchoring on costochondritis and dismissing cardiac/aortic causes despite the cocaine, pseudoephedrine, smoking, hypertension, and family history (-4); over-calling reproducible tenderness as proof it is benign in a higher-risk patient (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Costochondritis / musculoskeletal chest wall pain after heavy bench pressing (reproducible point tenderness, mechanical trigger, sharp pain worse with movement and palpation). However, this patient is NOT low-risk: cocaine use, high-dose pseudoephedrine, a 15-pack-year smoking history, hypertension, and a father with MI at 45 make sympathomimetic/cocaine-associated acute coronary syndrome or coronary vasospasm a genuine competing must-not-miss diagnosis that has to be actively excluded. Three (or more) dangerous mechanisms to exclude before committing: (1) cocaine/sympathomimetic ACS or coronary vasospasm, (2) aortic dissection (sympathomimetic surge plus uncontrolled hypertension), and (3) pulmonary embolism; also pericarditis/myocarditis and exertional pneumothorax. GERD (on omeprazole) and panic disorder are plausible non-cardiac contributors but are diagnoses of exclusion here. Reproducible tenderness lowers but does not by itself exclude a cardiac cause in this higher-risk man.

criticalActions.item: Cocaine/sympathomimetic-associated ACS or myocardial ischemia/vasospasm - actively excluded, not dismissed

coreActions.item: Names costochondritis with supporting features - reproducible point tenderness, exercise trigger, sharp pleuritic-with-movement quality
Names at least three dangerous mechanisms/etiologies to exclude (e.g. cocaine ACS/vasospasm, aortic dissection, PE, pericarditis/myocarditis, pneumothorax)
Recognizes GERD and panic disorder as plausible non-cardiac contributors given PMH/meds

bonusActions.item: Aortic dissection considered given sympathomimetic surge plus uncontrolled BP

penalties.item: Anchors on costochondritis and dismisses cardiac/aortic causes despite cocaine, pseudoephedrine, smoking, hypertension, and family history
Treats reproducible tenderness as proof of a benign cause in this higher-risk patient

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What workup do you want to order? Justify each test against this man's pretest probability - he is higher-risk than a typical young patient, but reproducible tenderness and a benign exam pull the other way.
steps.rubric: Serial high-sensitivity troponin (0/3h delta) to exclude cocaine-associated myocardial injury (CANT-MISS,4). 12-lead EKG, looking for ischemia AND for electrolyte/QT effects given his sympathomimetics and anticholinergic (CANT-MISS,3). CXR to screen for pneumothorax after heavy lifting and to assess the mediastinum (2). BMP - he is on a thiazide-free regimen but sympathomimetics shift potassium; checks K and renal function (2). Urine drug screen to confirm cocaine (1). CK acceptable to attribute the picture to skeletal muscle after heavy lifting (1). Considers D-dimer / PE risk stratification rather than reflexive CT (1). Recognizes this remains a largely clinical diagnosis and AVOIDS reflexive CT-angiography or stress testing once serial troponins and EKG are reassuring (reward restraint, 1). PENALTY: ordering CT coronary/stress test/CT-PA before the basic serial troponin and EKG, or a shotgun panel with no reasoning (-3); giving a beta-blocker for presumed cocaine chest pain (unopposed alpha) is a management error to avoid (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: serial high-sensitivity troponin (0- and 3-hour delta) to exclude cocaine-associated myocardial injury; 12-lead EKG read for ischemia AND for electrolyte/QT effects (cocaine + pseudoephedrine + anticholinergic hydroxyzine); CXR to screen for pneumothorax after heavy lifting and to look at the mediastinum/aorta; BMP (sympathomimetics shift K+; check potassium and renal function); urine drug screen to confirm cocaine; CK is reasonable to attribute the picture to skeletal muscle after heavy unaccustomed lifting. Risk-stratify for PE (e.g. D-dimer) rather than reflexively imaging. Once serial troponins and the EKG are reassuring this is a clinical diagnosis - avoid reflexive CT-angiography or stress testing. Do NOT give a beta-blocker for cocaine-associated chest pain.

criticalActions.item: Serial high-sensitivity troponin (0/3h delta) to exclude cocaine-associated myocardial injury
12-lead EKG read for ischemia AND electrolyte/QT effects

coreActions.item: CXR to screen for pneumothorax and assess the mediastinum/aorta
BMP to check potassium and renal function given sympathomimetics
Urine drug screen to confirm cocaine
CK to attribute the picture to skeletal muscle after heavy lifting

bonusActions.item: Risk-stratifies for PE (e.g. D-dimer) rather than reflexive CT-PA
Recognizes restraint - avoids reflexive CT-angiography/stress test once troponins and EKG reassure

penalties.item: CT coronary/stress test/CT-PA before basic serial troponin and EKG, or shotgun panel with no reasoning
Gives a beta-blocker for presumed cocaine chest pain (unopposed alpha)

requiredTests: Serial high-sensitivity troponin (0/3h delta) to exclude cocaine-associated myocardial injury
12-lead EKG read for ischemia AND electrolyte/QT effects
optionalTests: CXR to screen for pneumothorax and assess the mediastinum/aorta
BMP to check potassium and renal function given sympathomimetics
Urine drug screen to confirm cocaine
CK to attribute the picture to skeletal muscle after heavy lifting
contraindicatedTests: CT coronary/stress test/CT-PA before basic serial troponin and EKG, or shotgun panel with no reasoning
orderSensitiveActions: Serial high-sensitivity troponin (0/3h delta) to exclude cocaine-associated myocardial injury

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state how they fit (or do not fit) the clinical picture.
steps.rubric: Findings = U waves with flattened T waves and a long QU interval, consistent with HYPOKALEMIA (K 3.1) from the sympathomimetic surge; sinus rhythm without acute ST elevation or reciprocal ischemic change. CREDIT: identifies the U waves / hypokalemic pattern (7); connects it to the low potassium on the BMP and the cocaine/pseudoephedrine sympathomimetic state rather than calling it ischemia (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling the changes as a definite acute coronary syndrome, or missing the U waves entirely (-3).
keyFindings: connects it to the low potassium and sympathomimetic state, not ischemia
criticalFindings: U waves with flattened T waves (hypokalemia), no acute ischemic ST changes.
commonMistakes: over-calling the changes as a definite acute coronary syndrome
missing the U waves on the tracing
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm with U waves, flattened T waves, and a long QU interval - a hypokalemic pattern that matches the K of 3.1 on the BMP, driven by the cocaine and high-dose pseudoephedrine shifting potassium intracellularly. There is no ST elevation or reciprocal ischemic change. Correct the potassium and recheck; this is an electrolyte effect, not a primary coronary event.

criticalActions: U waves with flattened T waves (hypokalemia), no acute ischemic ST changes.
coreActions: connects it to the low potassium and sympathomimetic state, not ischemia

penalties.label: over-calls the changes as a definite ACS / misses the U waves

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = a single well-circumscribed solitary pulmonary nodule (~1.5 cm) in the mid lung zone, with NO pneumothorax and a normal mediastinal/aortic contour. CREDIT: identifies the solitary pulmonary nodule (4); states the pertinent negatives that matter here - no pneumothorax after heavy lifting and a normal mediastinum/aorta (3); recommends comparison with prior imaging and structured follow-up of the nodule given his smoking history (3). PENALTY: calling the film normal / dismissing the nodule as a nipple shadow without confirmation, or missing the pertinent negatives (-3).
keyFindings: states pertinent negatives: no pneumothorax, normal mediastinum/aorta
recommends comparison with priors / follow-up given smoking history
criticalFindings: Solitary pulmonary nodule; no pneumothorax; normal mediastinum.
commonMistakes: dismissing the nodule as a nipple shadow without confirmation
failing to comment on the absence of pneumothorax/mediastinal widening
steps.idealAnswer: A single well-circumscribed pulmonary nodule (~1.5 cm) in the mid lung zone. Pertinent negatives that matter in this man: no pneumothorax (he lifted heavily), and a normal mediastinal/aortic contour (no widening to suggest dissection). The nodule is incidental to today's pain but needs comparison with any prior imaging and structured follow-up, especially given his smoking history.

criticalActions: Solitary pulmonary nodule; no pneumothorax; normal mediastinum.
coreActions: states pertinent negatives: no pneumothorax, normal mediastinum/aorta
recommends comparison with priors / follow-up given smoking history

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the serial troponins, BMP, EKG, drug screen, and CXR, what is your leading diagnosis and reasoning? Name the single most discriminating piece of data, state any residual uncertainty, and explain how you reconciled the competing cardiac diagnosis.
steps.rubric: Costochondritis / musculoskeletal chest wall pain, with the dangerous competitors actively excluded (6). The most discriminating data: a normal, unchanged serial high-sensitivity troponin (0/3h delta) makes cocaine-associated myocardial injury/ACS very unlikely; reproducible point tenderness, the mechanical lifting trigger, a mildly elevated CK from skeletal muscle, and an EKG showing only hypokalemic U waves (no ischemia) all support a musculoskeletal cause (3). Names the discriminating test AND residual uncertainty - the cocaine and uncontrolled BP still warrant correcting the potassium, treating hypertension/anxiety, and counseling on cocaine cardiotoxicity, and the incidental lung nodule needs follow-up; would reconsider ischemia if pain becomes exertional or troponin rises (1).
steps.idealAnswer: Costochondritis / musculoskeletal chest wall pain. The single most discriminating result is the normal, unchanged serial high-sensitivity troponin (0/3h delta), which makes cocaine-associated myocardial injury or ACS very unlikely. This is reinforced by reproducible point tenderness over the costochondral junctions, the heavy-lifting trigger, a mildly elevated CK pointing to skeletal (not cardiac) muscle, and an EKG that shows only a hypokalemic U-wave pattern (explained by K 3.1 from the sympathomimetic surge) without ischemic ST change. Residual uncertainty and loose ends remain: the positive cocaine screen and uncontrolled BP mean I still correct the potassium, address the hypertension and anxiety, and counsel on cocaine cardiotoxicity; the incidental solitary pulmonary nodule needs structured follow-up given his smoking. I would re-escalate (repeat troponin, reconsider ischemia) if the pain becomes exertional or any troponin rises.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management and disposition plan? Be explicit about whether this patient can be safely discharged and what must be in place first.
steps.rubric: Per MGH chest pain algorithm (MSK/costochondritis once dangerous causes excluded): reassurance after serial troponins/EKG/CXR exclude ACS, dissection, PE, and pneumothorax (CANT-MISS,5); NSAIDs/acetaminophen for analgesia with activity modification and local heat (4); REPLETE the potassium (K 3.1) and recheck - do not ignore the electrolyte abnormality (4); explicit SAFE-DISCHARGE decision - this patient CAN be discharged home only after a reassuring 0/3h troponin delta and EKG, with clear return precautions, blood-pressure follow-up, and arranged follow-up for the incidental lung nodule (CANT-MISS,5); counsel on cocaine cardiotoxicity and offer cessation resources; counsel to STOP the high-dose pseudoephedrine; smoking-cessation counseling (3); medication reconciliation - flag the anticholinergic hydroxyzine and QT/sympathomimetic interactions, restart/optimize amlodipine for BP (2). PENALTY: giving a beta-blocker for cocaine-associated chest pain (unopposed alpha vasoconstriction) (-4); discharging WITHOUT excluding ACS / without return precautions / without nodule follow-up (-3); reflexive admission or CTA once serial troponins and EKG are reassuring (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Reassurance once serial troponins, EKG, and CXR have excluded ACS, aortic dissection, PE, and pneumothorax. Treat the costochondritis: NSAIDs or acetaminophen, activity modification, and local heat. Replete the potassium (K 3.1) and recheck it - do not ignore the electrolyte abnormality driving the U waves. SAFE DISCHARGE: this patient can go home, but ONLY after a reassuring 0/3h high-sensitivity troponin delta and EKG, with explicit return precautions (exertional pain, pressure, dyspnea, syncope), arranged primary-care follow-up for blood-pressure control, and structured follow-up of the incidental lung nodule. Counsel on cocaine cardiotoxicity with cessation resources, advise stopping the high-dose OTC pseudoephedrine, and offer smoking cessation. Do medication reconciliation: flag the anticholinergic hydroxyzine plus sympathomimetic QT effects and restart/optimize his amlodipine for hypertension. Critically, do NOT give a beta-blocker for cocaine-associated chest pain (risk of unopposed alpha vasoconstriction); use benzodiazepines and nitrates if needed for an active sympathomimetic syndrome.

criticalActions.item: Reassurance after serial troponins/EKG/CXR exclude ACS, dissection, PE, and pneumothorax
Explicit safe-discharge decision: home only after reassuring 0/3h troponin delta and EKG, with return precautions, BP follow-up, and lung-nodule follow-up

coreActions.item: NSAIDs/acetaminophen with activity modification and local heat
Counsel on cocaine cardiotoxicity/cessation, stop high-dose pseudoephedrine, smoking cessation
Medication reconciliation - flag anticholinergic hydroxyzine and QT/sympathomimetic interactions, optimize amlodipine for BP

bonusActions.item: Patient education on the benign course of costochondritis once cardiac causes are excluded

penalties.item: Gives a beta-blocker for cocaine-associated chest pain (unopposed alpha)
Discharges without excluding ACS / without return precautions / without nodule follow-up
Reflexive admission or CTA once serial troponins and EKG are reassuring

requiredActions: Reassurance after serial troponins/EKG/CXR exclude ACS, dissection, PE, and pneumothorax
Explicit safe-discharge decision: home only after reassuring 0/3h troponin delta and EKG, with return precautions, BP follow-up, and lung-nodule follow-up
optionalActions: NSAIDs/acetaminophen with activity modification and local heat
Counsel on cocaine cardiotoxicity/cessation, stop high-dose pseudoephedrine, smoking cessation
Medication reconciliation - flag anticholinergic hydroxyzine and QT/sympathomimetic interactions, optimize amlodipine for BP
Patient education on the benign course of costochondritis once cardiac causes are excluded
unsafeActions: Gives a beta-blocker for cocaine-associated chest pain (unopposed alpha)
Discharges without excluding ACS / without return precautions / without nodule follow-up
Reflexive admission or CTA once serial troponins and EKG are reassuring
steps.disposition: Discharge home after a reassuring 0/3h troponin delta and EKG, with return precautions and arranged PCP follow-up for BP and the lung nodule

physicalExamMappings.finding: Chest wall: REPRODUCIBLE point tenderness over the left 4th-5th costochondral junctions; firm palpation recreates his exact sharp pain. Mild tenderness of the pectoral muscle belly.
Cardiac: regular, normal S1/S2, no murmur, no rub, no gallop. No JVD.
Lungs: clear bilaterally, good air entry, no wheeze.
Abdomen: soft, mildly tender in the epigastrium (consistent with his reflux), no rebound.

Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness, symmetric pulses, no track marks.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 38M with GERD, panic disorder, hypertension, a 15-pack-year smoking history, and a strong family history of early MI presents with 3 days of sharp, reproducible left chest wall pain after heavy bench pressing, complicated by recent cocaine use and high-dose OTC pseudoephedrine. The diagnosis is Costochondritis, reached only after actively excluding the genuine competing diagnosis of cocaine/sympathomimetic-associated acute coronary syndrome. Serial high-sensitivity troponins (unchanged and normal), an EKG showing a hypokalemic U-wave pattern (K 3.1 from the sympathomimetic surge) rather than ischemia, a mildly elevated skeletal-muscle CK, and reproducible point tenderness all converge on a musculoskeletal cause. Multi-system findings (hypokalemia, positive cocaine screen, elevated CK, incidental lung nodule) must each be recognized and managed.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Chest wall: REPRODUCIBLE point tenderness over the left 4th-5th costochondral junctions; firm palpation recreates his exact sharp pain. Mild tenderness of the pectoral muscle belly.
Cardiac: regular, normal S1/S2, no murmur, no rub, no gallop. No JVD.
Lungs: clear bilaterally, good air entry, no wheeze.
Abdomen: soft, mildly tender in the epigastrium (consistent with his reflux), no rebound.
Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness, symmetric pulses, no track marks.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals - note the elevated BP and family history.
Elicit the focused history, drawing out the cocaine use, pseudoephedrine overuse, smoking, and family history only on respectful direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam, palpating the chest wall to reproduce the pain and checking for cardiac, pulmonary, abdominal, and vascular signs.
Build a differential led by Costochondritis but holding cocaine/sympathomimetic ACS, dissection, and PE as genuine must-not-miss competitors.
Order serial troponins, an EKG, BMP, CXR, and drug screen; interpret the hypokalemic U-wave EKG and the incidental lung nodule on CXR.
Commit to Costochondritis after a reassuring 0/3h troponin delta, replete potassium, and arrange safe discharge with return precautions, BP follow-up, nodule follow-up, and substance-use counseling.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on reproducible tenderness and dismissing cardiac/aortic causes despite cocaine, pseudoephedrine, smoking, hypertension, and family history
Giving a beta-blocker for cocaine-associated chest pain (unopposed alpha vasoconstriction)
Discharging without excluding ACS, without return precautions, or without lung-nodule follow-up
Ignoring the hypokalemia driving the U waves
managementPearls: Reassurance after serial troponins/EKG/CXR exclude ACS, dissection, PE, and pneumothorax
Explicit safe-discharge decision with return precautions, BP follow-up, and lung-nodule follow-up
criticalWorkupItems: Serial high-sensitivity troponin (0/3h delta) to exclude cocaine-associated myocardial injury
12-lead EKG read for ischemia AND electrolyte/QT effects
criticalInterpretationItems: U waves with flattened T waves (hypokalemia), no acute ischemic ST changes.
Solitary pulmonary nodule; no pneumothorax; normal mediastinum.
criticalManagementItems: Reassurance after serial troponins/EKG/CXR exclude ACS, dissection, PE, and pneumothorax
Explicit safe-discharge decision: home only after reassuring 0/3h troponin delta and EKG, with return precautions, BP follow-up, and lung-nodule follow-up

Solitary pulmonary nodule; no pneumothorax; normal mediastinum.
imageInterpretationPearls: U waves with flattened T waves and a long QU interval are the EKG signature of hypokalemia - tie the tracing back to the serum potassium, do not call it ischemia.
An incidental solitary pulmonary nodule is an interpretation task in its own right - name it, state the pertinent negatives (no pneumothorax, normal mediastinum), then arrange follow-up.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's fear of a heart attack
Asks about substance use respectfully and non-judgmentally before sensitive questions
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language

libraryUpgrade.imagePolicy: integrated read (EKG pinned to LITFL hypokalemia study); pathology-forward CXR
